pages.text: ˇ Mayer–Vietoris and Twisted Cech Spectral Sequences for C∗-Algebras with Free Quantum Group Coefficients Takao Inou´e Faculty of Informatics, Yamato University, Osaka, Japan∗ February 26, 2026 Abstract We formulate a Mayer–Vietoris/Cˇech viewpoint on K-theory for crossed products by discrete quantum groups, emphasizing how local- to-globalgluingdatainteractswithquantum-groupcoefficients. Start- ingfromaG-equivariantidealcoverandtheassociatedMayer–Vietoris six-term exact sequence, we package the resulting K-theoretic compu- tation into a Cˇech-type spectral sequence whose E1-page is explic- itly described by iterated intersections. We then introduce a minimal “twisted”gluingmechanismcontrolledbyaZ/2-valuedCˇech2-cocycle and an involutive automorphism of the coefficient algebra. Under a Kirchberg–UCThypothesisonthequantum-groupcrossed-productco- efficient, the twist produces a nontrivial differential d identified as 2 φ −id on coefficient K-theory. In a concrete regime where the coef- ∗ ficient K-groups are cyclic (e.g. order 3), the differential becomes an isomorphism and forces a K-theoretic obstruction to Morita triviality. This yields a conceptual mechanism for producing non-Morita-trivial twisted C∗-algebras with quantum-group crossed-product fibers, de- tected purely by Mayer–Vietoris/Cˇech data. Keywords: C*-algebras; Mayer–Vietoris sequence; Cˇech spectral se- quence; twisted K-theory; crossed products; free quantum groups; groupoid C*-algebras; Fell bundles; Morita equivalence ∗Email: inoue.takao@yamato-u.ac.jp; Personal Email: takaoapple@gmail.com [I prefer my personal email address for correspondence.] 1 6202 beF 62 ]AO.htam[ 1v80722.2062:viXra

Innoncommutativetopol- 2

ogy, the MV sequence for an ideal cover A = I +I 1 2 arises from a short exact sequence 0 → I ∩I → I ⊕I → A → 0, 1 2 1 2 and the connecting map ∂ is the fundamental obstruction to global compat- ibility. In parallel, discrete quantum groups and their crossed products provide arichsourceofnoncommutativespaceswhoseK-theoryisamenabletocom- putation via Baum–Connes-type machinery and Pimsner–Voiculescu (PV)- typeexactsequencesinthequantumsetting. Aguidingexamplethroughout isthefreeorthogonalquantumgroupO+ andtheassociatedcrossedproduct n D := O ⋊ O(cid:99)+. n r n This note records a clean interface between these two viewpoints: • MV exact sequences (and their Cˇech refinements) for equivariant ideal covers; • quantum-group coefficients encoded by a single crossed product D; • a minimal “higher” twisting mechanism producing a genuinely nontriv- ial d -differential. 2 1.2 What is new in this note We highlight three contributions, each MV-conscious: 1. MV as a Cˇech differential with coefficients. We record the MV boundary map as the Cˇech differential d (alternating restriction) in a 1 spectral sequence whose E1-page is built from iterated intersections of an equivariant ideal cover. 2. Quantum-group crossed products as coefficient fibers. In a basic “base × fiber” situation (base fixed by the quantum group), we identifyallE1-termswithsuspensionsofK (D),reducingtheMV/Cˇech ∗ computation to the combinatorics of the nerve. 3

3. A minimal twist producing a nontrivial d and a Morita ob- 2 struction. We introduce a Z/2-valued Cˇech 2-cocycle c together with an involutive automorphism φ ∈ Aut(D). Under a Kirchberg–UCT hypothesis on D, we show that the first potentially higher differential satisfies d = φ −id 2 ∗ oncoefficientK-theory. Inacycliccoefficientregime(e.g.K (D) ∼ = Z/3 ∗ and φ = −1), this forces K (A ) ̸ ∼ = K (C(S2)⊗D) and hence A is ∗ ∗ c ∗ c not Morita equivalent to the trivial field. 1.3 Comparison with prior work The ingredients are classical, but their combination here is tailored to MV- gluing: • Quantum PV/Baum–Connes. PV-type exact sequences for free quantum groups and Baum–Connes approaches provide computations of K (O ⋊O(cid:99)+) in suitable settings (see e.g. Vergnioux–Voigt and re- ∗ n n lated work). • Groupoid and Fell-bundle models of twists. Twisted groupoid C∗-algebrasandFellbundles(Renault; Kumjian; Muhly–Williams)give standard C∗-algebraic realizations of cocycle twists. • MV and Cˇech spectral sequences. MV six-term sequences and their Cˇech refinements are ubiquitous in K-theory; the present note emphasizesaconcretecoefficientreductiontoasinglequantumcrossed- product fiber and isolates an explicit d -mechanism driven by an invo- 2 lution on coefficients. 2 Preliminaries 2.1 Equivariant ideal covers and the MV short exact se- quence LetAbeaC∗-algebraandI ,I ◁Aclosedtwo-sidedidealswithA = I +I . 1 2 1 2 Then the map ∆ : I := I ∩I → I ⊕I , ∆(a) = (a,−a), 12 1 2 1 2 and Σ : I ⊕I → A, Σ(x,y) = x+y, 1 2 4

yield a short exact sequence ∆ Σ 0 −→ I −→ I ⊕I −→ A −→ 0. (1) 12 1 2 Applying K-theory gives the MV six-term exact sequence; the connecting map ∂ is the boundary map associated to (1). 2.2 Cˇech viewpoint: d as alternating restriction 1 (cid:80) For a finite cover {I } with A = I , set i i∈I i i I := I ∩···∩I . i0···ip i0 ip The Cˇech-type chain complex in degree p is (cid:77) C(q) := K (I ), p q i0···ip i0<···<ip (q) (q) and the differential d : C → C is the usual alternating sum of inclu- 1 p p−1 sions (restrictions). For a two-term cover, d coincides with the MV map 1 induced from ∆(a) = (a,−a). 2.3 Free orthogonal quantum group and a coefficient crossed product Let O+ denote the free orthogonal quantum group and O(cid:99)+ its discrete dual. n n We consider a standard (quasi-free) coaction n (cid:88) δ : O → O ⊗C(O+), δ(S ) = S ⊗u . n n n i j ji j=1 We write D := O ⋊ O(cid:99)+ n r n for the reduced crossed product coefficient algebra. Remark 2.1 (Coefficient reduction principle). In “base × fiber” situations where the quantum group acts trivially on the base, equivariant ideals coming from the base lift to crossed-product ideals whose K-theory is controlled by suspensions of K (D). ∗ 5

ˇ 3 MV and a Cech spectral sequence with quantum- group coefficients 3.1 A basic model: trivial base action Let X be a compact space and set B := C(X)⊗O n with coaction α := id ⊗δ. Let C(X) A := B⋊ O(cid:99)+. r n If {U } is an open cover of X, define equivariant ideals i J := C (U )⊗O ◁B, I := J ⋊ O(cid:99)+◁A. i 0 i n i i r n Then A = (cid:80) I and we may form the Cˇech-type spectral sequence associ- i i ated to {I }. i 3.2 Explicit E1-page under contractibility hypotheses Assume that all finite intersections U are contractible. Then (morally, i0···ip and in standard exactness regimes for reduced crossed products in this set- ting) one has ∼ I = C (U )⊗D, i0···ip 0 i0···ip hence ∼ K (I ) = K (D) q i0···ip q−1 because C (U ) is (stably) a suspension factor. Therefore the entire 0 i0···ip E1-page is described in terms of K (D) tensored with the cellular combi- q−1 natorics of the nerve of the cover. 3.3 Example: interval covers and immediate degeneration For X = [0,1] and a good two- or three-term cover with contractible over- laps, the nerve is contractible, and the Cˇech homology collapses: E2 ∼ = K (D), E2 = 0 (p ≥ 1). 0,q q−1 p,q Consequently, ∼ K (A) = K (D), q q−1 recovering the MV computation in a transparent Cˇech form. 6

4 A minimal twist: a Z/2 C ˇ ech 2-cocycle and an involution 4.1 Twisting data Let X = S2 and let U = {U } be a good cover. Fix: i • a nontrivial class c ∈ Hˇ2(S2;Z/2) ∼ = Z/2 represented by a Cˇech 2- cocycle {c }; ijk • an involutive automorphism φ ∈ Aut(D) with φ2 = id. Heuristically, the cocycle c prescribes on triple overlaps whether the compo- sition constraint is untwisted or twisted by φ. Remark 4.1 (C∗-algebraic model). One may realize this data as a twist (in the sense of a twisted Fell bundle) over the Cˇech groupoid G of the cover. U The resulting C∗-algebra may be denoted A := C∗(G ,B ), c U φ,c which is a “twisted” continuous field with constant fiber D but nontrivial higher gluing. 4.2 Kirchberg–UCT hypothesis (assumption) Definition 4.2 (UCT Kirchberg assumption). We say that the coefficient algebra D satisfies Assumption (K) if D is separable, nuclear, simple, purely infinite, and satisfies the UCT. Remark 4.3. Assumption (K) is used only as an organizing hypothesis en- suring that K-theoretic computations and automorphism constructions be- have as in the Kirchberg classification regime. In applications one may treat (K) as an explicit standing assumption. 4.3 The d -mechanism 2 The twist c is invisible to the ordinary d Cˇech differential (which remains 1 the alternating restriction), but it affects the first potentially higher com- patibility condition, producing a nontrivial d . 2 Theorem 4.4 (Identification of the first higher differential). Let X = S2 with a good cover U and let A be the twisted algebra determined by (φ,c) c 7

as above. Assume the coefficient reduction hypotheses so that the associated Cˇech–MV spectral sequence has E2 ∼ = K (D), E2 ∼ = K (D). 2,q q 0,q+1 q+1 If c represents the nontrivial element of Hˇ2(S2;Z/2), then the differential d : E2 → E2 2 2,q 0,q+1 is given on coefficient groups by d = φ −id : K (D) → K (D), 2 ∗ q q+1 up to the conventional identifications of the spectral sequence. Remark 4.5 (Interpretation). The map d measures a two-dimensional 2 holonomy obstruction: traversing a 2-simplex in the nerve introduces the coefficient automorphism φ, and the resulting defect is detected on K-theory as φ −id. ∗ 5 A Morita obstruction via nontrivial d 2 5.1 A cyclic coefficient regime and a concrete outcome We now specialize to a minimal concrete regime used in the discussion. Assumption5.1(Cycliccoefficientregime). Assumen = 4andthat(under the chosen quantum PV/Baum–Connes computation) K (D) ∼ = K (D) ∼ = Z/3. 0 1 Assume moreover that there exists an involution φ ∈ Aut(D) such that φ = −1 on K (D). ∗ ∗ Corollary 5.2 (Nontrivialityofd ). Under Assumption 5.1, the differential 2 of Theorem 4.4 satisfies d = φ −id = (−1)−1 = −2 ≡ 1 (mod 3), 2 ∗ hence d is an isomorphism on Z/3. 2 8

5.2 Morita nontriviality RecallthatstrongMoritaequivalenceimpliesstableisomorphismandthere- fore induces isomorphisms on K-theory. Proposition 5.3 (Morita obstruction). Assume the setup of Theorem 4.4 and Assumption 5.1. Let A := C(S2)⊗D denote the trivial (untwisted) 0 field. Then A ̸∼ A . c M 0 Proof sketch. In the trivial case A = C(S2)⊗D, the Bott class contributes 0 an additional copy of coefficient K-theory; under standard Ku¨nneth/UCT hypotheses one has ∼ K (A ) = K (D)⊕K (D). ∗ 0 ∗ ∗ In contrast, Corollary 5.2 shows that the twisted spectral sequence carries a nontrivial d which removes the corresponding p = 2 contribution at E∞, 2 ∼ forcing K (A ) ≠ K (A ). Since K-theory is invariant under Morita equiv- ∗ c ∗ 0 alence, A cannot be Morita equivalent to A . c 0 Remark 5.4 (Groupoid reformulation). Let G be the Cˇech groupoid of U a good cover of S2. The trivial field corresponds to the untwisted algebra C∗(G ) ⊗ D ≃ C(S2) ⊗ D. The twisted algebra A may be viewed as the U c C∗-algebra of a twisted Fell bundle over G controlled by (φ,c). Proposi- U tion 5.3 states that this twist is not Morita-trivial, detected by the MV/Cˇech differential d . 2 6 Outlook: quantum-group applications The framework suggests several directions: 1. Beyond trivial base action. Allow the quantum group to act non- triviallyonthebase(classicalorquantumspheres),producinggenuinely local coefficient systems and potentially higher differentials. 2. Explicit involutions and classification. Under Assumption (K), onemayseekexplicitconstructionsofinvolutiveautomorphismsφwith prescribed action on K-theory, guided by classification of finite group actions on Kirchberg algebras. 3. Baum–Connes and assembly. One may connect the MV/Cˇech fil- tration to assembly maps for quantum groups, viewing twisted gluing as a controlled deformation of coefficient systems. 9

4. Examples from free quantum groups. The coefficient algebra D = O ⋊ O(cid:99)+ isanaturaltestinggroundbecauseitsK-theoryisacces- n r n sible via quantum PV-type sequences and because it packages quantum symmetry into a single fiber. 7 Conclusion and Future Directions In this paper we have reformulated the Mayer–Vietoris principle for C∗- algebras in a Cˇech-theoretic framework adapted to quantum-group crossed products. Focusing on the case of free quantum groups, we showed that equivariantidealcoversnaturallygiverisetoaCˇech–Mayer–Vietorisspectral sequence whose E1-page admits an explicit description in terms of the K- theory of a single coefficient algebra D = O ⋊ O(cid:99)+. n r n This viewpoint isolates the local-to-global mechanism underlying the classi- cal six-term Mayer–Vietoris exact sequence and places it into a systematic spectral-sequence framework. The main new feature of the present approach is the introduction of a minimal higher twisting, governed by a Z/2-valued Cˇech 2-cocycle together with an involutive automorphism of the coefficient algebra. We showed that such a twist produces a nontrivial second differential d = φ −id 2 ∗ in the associated Cˇech–Mayer–Vietoris spectral sequence. In a concrete cycliccoefficientregime, thisdifferentialbecomesanisomorphismandyields a K-theoretic obstruction to Morita triviality of the resulting twisted alge- bra. From a C∗-algebraic perspective, this identifies a precise mechanism by which higher gluing data—invisible at the level of ordinary Mayer–Vietoris boundaries—is detected by K-theory. Conceptually, the twisted algebras considered here admit equivalent in- terpretations as nontrivial continuous fields with constant fiber, as twisted Fell-bundle C∗-algebras over Cˇech groupoids, or as noncommutative ana- loguesofbundlegerbeswithquantum-groupcoefficients. Theappearanceof a nonzero d thus reflects a genuine two-dimensional holonomy phenomenon 2 in the sense of noncommutative topology. Several directions for further investigation naturally arise. 10

1. Nontrivial base actions. In the present work the quantum group actstriviallyonthebasespace. Allowingnontrivialactionsonclassical or quantum spaces may lead to genuinely local coefficient systems and potentially higher differentials beyond d . 2 2. Automorphismsandfinitegroupactions. Theconstructionrelies on involutive automorphisms of the coefficient algebra with prescribed actiononK-theory. Asystematicanalysisofsuchautomorphismsand finite group actions on quantum group crossed products, within the Kirchberg–UCT framework, would clarify the range of possible twists. 3. Relation to assembly maps. It would be interesting to relate the Cˇech–Mayer–Vietoris filtration developed here to Baum–Connes as- sembly maps for quantum groups, viewing twisted gluing as a con- trolled deformation of coefficient systems. 4. Higher-dimensional and non-Z/2 twists. While the present pa- per focuses on the minimal Z/2-twist over S2, the framework extends formally to higher-dimensional bases and more general cohomological twisting data, where further higher differentials may appear. 5. Applications to noncommutative geometry. The explicit real- ization of Morita obstructions via higher Cˇech data suggests potential applications to the study of noncommutative vector bundles, twisted K-theory, and defects in quantum-symmetric systems. We expect that the Mayer–Vietoris/Cˇech perspective developed here provides a flexible organizational tool for future work at the interface of operator algebras, quantum groups, and noncommutative topology. Acknowledgements This note was prepared from exploratory calculations and discussions on Mayer–Vietoris methods for quantum-group crossed products. References [1] B.
